summary: The Gemara resolves the R. Meir/R. Yehuda dispute about wood arrangements on the altar, analyzing the verse 'and the fire on the altar shall be kept burning' to determine how many distinct arrangements are required and what each serves.

display.whats: How does R. Meir resolve the question of wood arrangements, and what does 'the fire on the altar shall be kept burning' add?
display.hint: The Gemara analyzes how R. Meir uses the repeated mention of 'fire on the altar' to derive an additional wood arrangement. Each mention yields one arrangement. R. Meir needs five; R. Yehuda four. The verse 'it shall not be extinguished' adds the prohibition against extinguishing, not a new arrangement.
display.title: 'It Shall Not Be Extinguished': A Prohibition, Not a New Pyre

learning.ahaMoment: Every 'fire on the altar' phrase in Torah creates a new wood arrangement. Count the mentions, count the arrangements. R. Meir finds one more than R. Yehuda.
learning.memoryAnchor: The fire prohibition ('lo tichbeh') is not a new arrangement but a prohibition against extinguishing what already exists.
learning.learnerQuestion: Does the verse 'the fire on the altar shall be kept burning, it shall not be extinguished' support R. Meir's fifth wood arrangement, or does it mean something else entirely?
learning.coreTension: R. Meir reads added phrases about the perpetual fire as justifying an extra wood arrangement, but the Gemara must determine whether 'lo tichbeh' (it shall not be extinguished) is really describing a new arrangement requirement or is simply a prohibition against letting the fire go out.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara works through the verse phrase by phrase and concludes that 'lo tichbeh' is a prohibition against extinguishing the fire, not a directive to add a new wood arrangement, so the verse does not support R. Meir's fifth arrangement - the final ruling follows R. Yehuda's four.

takeaway.text: 'It shall not be extinguished' (lo tichbeh) is a prohibition against letting the altar fire go out, not a command to add a new wood arrangement; the final ruling follows R. Yehuda that four arrangements suffice.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may read 'lo tichbeh' as describing an additional wood-arrangement requirement because it appears alongside the wood-arrangement verses, missing that the Gemara identifies it as a separate prohibition about maintaining the fire.

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: 'And the fire on the altar shall be kept burning' - this phrase teaches the arrangement must be maintained, not just lit once.
argumentFlow.label: Derivation: 'And the fire on the altar shall

argumentFlow.speaker: R. Meir/R. Yehuda
argumentFlow.text: R. Meir: an additional fire mention creates a fifth arrangement for Yom Kippur coals.
argumentFlow.label: Dispute: R. Meir: an additional fire mention creates

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: R. Yehuda: the Yom Kippur coal fire is the same as the incense fire - no fifth needed.
argumentFlow.label: Resolution: R. Yehuda: the Yom Kippur coal fire

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: 'It shall not be extinguished' is a prohibition, not a new arrangement requirement.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: 'It shall not be extinguished' is a

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Additional altar fire mention noted; Gemara checks whether it creates another arrangement.
argumentFlow.label: Narrative: Additional altar fire mention noted; Gemara checks

misconceptions.misconception: The phrase 'it shall not be extinguished' is part of the verse's instructions for how many wood arrangements to build on the altar.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara reads 'lo tichbeh' as a prohibition against letting the fire go out, not as a new wood-arrangement requirement; the final ruling follows R. Yehuda's four arrangements.

visualizableElements.label: altar fire verse 'lo tichbeh' as prohibition not arrangement

quizSeeds.answer: It is a prohibition against extinguishing the altar fire, not a requirement for a new separate wood arrangement.
quizSeeds.question: What does 'lo tichbeh' (it shall not be extinguished) teach about the altar fire?

sugyot.finalRuling: The altar fire prohibition 'lo tichbeh' is not a new arrangement but a ban on extinguishing the existing fire; R. Yehuda holds four arrangements suffice.

glossary.en: It shall not be extinguished - the Torah prohibition against allowing the altar fire to go out (Lev. 6:6).

rashiTranslations.en: The altar must have the fire arranged on it - not just lit but structured as a pyre.
R. Meir and R. Yehuda disagree about how many times 'fire on the altar' is mentioned distinctly.
Each mention of 'fire on the altar' in Torah creates one wood arrangement requirement.
R. Yehuda counts four mentions; R. Meir counts five.
The fifth mention (according to R. Meir) creates a separate arrangement for Yom Kippur incense coals.
R. Yehuda holds the Yom Kippur coals come from the second arrangement (incense fire).
The verse 'the fire on the altar shall be burning in it' - in it means on the altar itself, not inside.
'Shall be burning' teaches continuous burning, not intermittent.
'It shall not be extinguished' is parsed separately - it is a prohibition, not a positive requirement.
A positive requirement would demand relighting; a prohibition demands not extinguishing.
The distinction matters: violating lo tichbeh is prohibited even before the fire goes out.
A priest who pours water on the altar fire violates lo tichbeh even if a coal remains.
The Gemara: R. Meir would say this verse adds to the count, R. Yehuda would not.
At any rate, everyone agrees there is at least one additional mention that must be explained.
The extra mention is then assigned to an existing arrangement by R. Yehuda or a new one by R. Meir.
The wood festival families are mentioned here in connection with who supplied the altar wood.
Families who donated wood had the privilege of burning it on the altar on their appointed day.
This was considered an honor and was hereditary within certain priestly families.
The Gemara notes the exact number of wood donations per year and which families contributed.
The arrangement of wood had to be done in the morning as part of the daily service.
Priests drew lots for who would arrange the wood on the altar each day.
The wood arrangement came before the removal of ash (terumat ha-deshen).
Wait - actually ash removal came first, then the wood arrangement in the correct order.
45b concludes the wood arrangement discussion and the dispute is partially resolved.
The final ruling follows R. Yehuda: four arrangements suffice.
The Gemara will now move to bar Kappara's teaching about R. Meir's opinion.
R. Meir's view is cited in the name of bar Kappara who attributed it to him.
The attribution chain: bar Kappara said in the name of R. Meir.
The Gemara questions what bar Kappara adds by citing R. Meir specifically.
